scenario_results.reason: Policy hierarchy guidance stays narrow, identifies missing ownership data, and avoids pretending the hierarchy is complete without supporting context.
Traceability outputs preserve source-to-standard linkage, evidence expectations, and confidence notes instead of forcing unsupported exact mappings.
Backlog prioritization is explicitly risk-based and accounts for evidence weakness, exception drift, and dependency sequencing.
Commercial-term reasoning is grounded in official provider terms and translated into standards guidance without drifting into negotiation or legal advice.
Exception review requires traceable evidence and compensating-control support before any favorable recommendation.
Conflicting obligations trigger low-confidence escalation to legal or compliance owners rather than a guessed harmonization.
Procurement negotiation work is rejected clearly and routed to the adjacent sourcing or legal lane.
License optimization and entitlement strategy are rejected as adjacent software-licensing work while bounded policy guidance remains available.
Controls testing execution and sign-off are rejected cleanly with routing to testing and audit owners.
Mixed policy and implementation requests are split safely so technical delivery work is not absorbed into this specialist.
Shared meta-agent outages fall back safely to the local reviewed baseline with freshness limits and no unsupported new claims.
Deployment and marketing notes remain consistent with the narrow boundary, evaluation evidence, and validated repository contract.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the issue #124 requirements, including policy analysis, control traceability, commercial-term reasoning, risk-based prioritization, out-of-scope rejection, low-confidence escalation, and shared-meta-agent fallback behavior.
No unreviewed regressions remain and human verification was recorded in the readiness evidence.
